does: This paper proposes an integrated red teaming framework that uses large language models themselves to generate attack prompts through in-context learning, combining the efficiency of automatic methods with the quality of manual approaches. The authors develop an attack framework that instructs LLMs to mimic human-generated adversarial prompts, then introduce a complementary defense framework that fine-tunes victim LLMs through iterative interactions with the attack framework to improve their robustness against such attacks. The work validates both frameworks across multiple LLMs and releases the SAP dataset containing attack prompts of varying scales, establishing a benchmark resource for safety evaluation and improvement.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial robustness of language models, building on prior red teaming research that has relied on either labor-intensive manual prompt engineering or limited automatic generation methods. It responds to the tension between construction cost and quality that has constrained previous approaches, leveraging recent advances in in-context learning and prompt instruction-following capabilities of modern LLMs. The paper builds on established ideas in adversarial machine learning and safety evaluation while incorporating insights from both human-crafted attacks and automated vulnerability discovery, positioning itself within the broader effort to understand and mitigate harmful outputs in large language models alongside contemporaneous work on alignment and safety.
enables: This framework establishes a scalable pathway for continuous red teaming and defense cycles, enabling more rapid identification of safety vulnerabilities in LLMs at lower cost than manual approaches. The released SAP dataset facilitates standardized safety benchmarking across different models and enables future work on adversarial robustness evaluation. The iterative attack-defense loop points toward automated safety improvement pipelines where models can be progressively hardened against adversarial prompts, potentially informing broader alignment and safety training methodologies. This work also enables investigation of transferability of attack prompts across models and exploration of what linguistic patterns trigger harmful behaviors, supporting the development of more sophisticated safety interventions and understanding of LLM vulnerabilities.
